title: Unstable Bradycardia During Laparoscopic Insufflation
summary: A healthy 34-year-old woman undergoing laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy becomes profoundly bradycardic as the surgeon insufflates the abdomen — a vagal reflex from peritoneal stretch. Learners must recognize the trigger, have the surgeon release the pneumoperitoneum, give atropine per ACLS, and run the asystole algorithm if the rhythm degenerates.
learningObjectives: Recognize progressive bradycardia on the ECG and pleth before the blood pressure cuff confirms hypotension, and connect it to peritoneal insufflation as the vagal trigger.
Treat the cause first: ask the surgeon to release the pneumoperitoneum and stop stimulation while drugs are drawn up.
Apply the ACLS bradycardia algorithm: atropine 1 mg IV repeated every 3-5 minutes (maximum 3 mg), then transcutaneous pacing and/or an epinephrine infusion 2-10 mcg/min if refractory.
Declare cardiac arrest and start high-quality CPR immediately when the rhythm degenerates to asystole, giving epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3-5 minutes per the non-shockable pathway.
Use capnography during CPR: EtCO2 reflects compression quality, and an abrupt sustained rise heralds return of spontaneous circulation.
Communicate as a team: call for help early, assign roles, and make a joint post-event plan with the surgeon.
setup: OR configured for gynecologic laparoscopy: patient supine, ETT in place, mechanical ventilation, sevoflurane running, surgeon about to insufflate.
Labeled syringes: atropine 0.4 mg/mL, epinephrine 10 mcg/mL and 100 mcg/mL dilutions, glycopyrrolate (slower onset — a reasonable but inferior choice for unstable bradycardia).
Code cart with defibrillator/transcutaneous pacer immediately outside the room; pacing pads available.
Confederate surgeon who announces 'insufflating now' on cue, can report intra-abdominal pressure if asked, and releases the pneumoperitoneum only when explicitly asked to.
NIBP is set to cycle every 2 minutes — remind faculty that the displayed pressure lags the crisis; the ECG rate and pleth tell the real-time story.
Anesthesia record shows an uneventful induction 15 minutes ago; fentanyl 100 mcg at induction (opioids potentiate vagal tone).

patient.name: Elena Marsh
pmh: Symptomatic right ovarian cyst scheduled for laparoscopic cystectomy
Distance runner with high resting vagal tone; resting heart rate in the high 50s
No cardiac history; normal preoperative ECG

airway.notes: Grade 1 view at intubation; 7.0 ETT secured at 21 cm; already intubated at scenario start.

patient.plan: Laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy under general endotracheal anesthesia with sevoflurane maintenance; pneumoperitoneum to 15 mmHg.

phases.label: Insufflation
phases.description: Pneumoperitoneum established; CO2 absorption and peritoneal stretch begin.

phases.label: Bradycardia
phases.description: Vagally mediated heart rate collapse with hypotension.

phases.label: Arrest
phases.description: Asystole if the reflex is not interrupted — CPR and ACLS.

phases.label: Treatment
phases.description: Desufflation, atropine, escalation to pacing or epinephrine.

phases.label: Recovery
phases.description: Rhythm and pressure restored; team debriefs the plan for the case.

events.label: Pneumoperitoneum insufflation begins
events.description: The surgeon insufflates CO2 to 15 mmHg. EtCO2 drifts up from absorption and blood pressure rises slightly from increased afterload. This is the exposure to the vagal trigger.

events.label: Vagal bradycardia onset
events.description: Peritoneal stretch triggers a vagal reflex: heart rate falls into the 40s over a minute with drifting blood pressure. The NIBP has not cycled yet — the ECG and pleth carry the story.

events.label: Profound unstable bradycardia
events.description: Heart rate falls to the low 30s with SBP in the 70s — unstable bradycardia by ACLS criteria (hypotension with bradycardia). Without desufflation and atropine this will degenerate.

events.label: Asystole
events.description: The rhythm degenerates to asystole: flat line, no pulse, pleth lost, EtCO2 collapses. Faculty-triggered if the vagal stimulus is not removed and atropine is delayed. Requires immediate CPR and epinephrine 1 mg IV per the non-shockable algorithm.

events.label: High-quality CPR in progress
events.description: Good compressions generate an EtCO2 in the mid-teens. Fire once compressions start; remind learners that EtCO2 below 10 mmHg suggests inadequate compression quality.

events.label: Pneumoperitoneum released
events.description: The surgeon desufflates on request. With the vagal stimulus removed the heart rate begins to recover toward the high 40s — partial improvement while drugs take effect.

events.label: Atropine 1 mg IV response
events.description: Atropine 1 mg IV (ACLS 2020 dose; repeat every 3-5 min to a 3 mg maximum) lifts the heart rate to the 70s and restores the pressure over about 90 seconds.

events.label: Epinephrine infusion response
events.description: For atropine-refractory bradycardia, an epinephrine infusion 2-10 mcg/min raises the rate to the high 80s and supports the pressure.

events.label: Transcutaneous pacing with capture
events.description: Pacing pads placed and electrical capture confirmed at a set rate of 80/min with a corresponding pulse (the monitor shows a regular rhythm at the paced rate).

events.label: ROSC after CPR and epinephrine
events.description: An abrupt sustained rise in EtCO2 heralds return of spontaneous circulation; a slow sinus rhythm returns with a palpable pulse. Fire after effective CPR plus arrest-dose epinephrine.

events.label: Stabilization achieved
events.description: Heart rate and pressure normalize; EtCO2 settles. The team discusses whether to proceed (typically yes, with slow re-insufflation at lower pressure and vagolytic cover) and documents the event.

expectedActions.label: Recognize progressive bradycardia from the ECG/pleth and link it to insufflation
expectedActions.description: The NIBP lags — the diagnosis is made from the falling ECG rate, not the cuff.

expectedActions.label: Ask the surgeon to release the pneumoperitoneum and stop stimulation
expectedActions.description: Removing the vagal stimulus is the first and fastest treatment.

expectedActions.label: Give atropine 1 mg IV promptly, repeating every 3-5 min (max 3 mg)
Ventilate with 100% oxygen and verify the airway and EtCO2
Call for help and the code cart when the bradycardia is unstable
Prepare transcutaneous pacing and/or an epinephrine infusion (2-10 mcg/min) for atropine-refractory bradycardia
Declare asystole and start high-quality CPR without delay
Give epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3-5 minutes per the non-shockable algorithm
Run the arrest cleanly: 2-minute cycles, minimal pauses, EtCO2 to gauge compression quality
Consider reversible causes (vagal stimulus, hypovolemia, hypoxia, embolism, deep anesthetic)
Make a joint plan with the surgeon (proceed vs abort, re-insufflation strategy) and arrange post-ROSC care if arrest occurred

expectedProgression: Stable maintenance anesthesia; the surgeon insufflates about a minute in, with a mild EtCO2 rise as the only change.
Around 3 minutes, the heart rate slides into the 40s — the cuff has not cycled, so learners must read the ECG and pleth.
By about 5-6 minutes the rate is in the low 30s with SBP in the 70s: unstable bradycardia.
Learners should announce the problem, ask for desufflation, go to 100% oxygen, give atropine 1 mg IV, and call for help.
Desufflation plus atropine recovers the rate and pressure over 1-2 minutes; refractory cases need pacing or an epinephrine infusion.
If the stimulus continues untreated, faculty fires asystole: CPR, epinephrine 1 mg IV, 2-minute cycles until ROSC.
After stabilization the team decides how to resume the case — typically slow, lower-pressure re-insufflation with atropine available.
correctManagement: Treat the cause first: ask the surgeon to release the pneumoperitoneum and pause stimulation the moment the vagal pattern is recognized.
Ventilate with 100% oxygen and confirm the airway, EtCO2, and depth of anesthesia.
Give atropine 1 mg IV for unstable bradycardia; repeat every 3-5 minutes to a maximum of 3 mg (ACLS 2020).
If atropine is ineffective: transcutaneous pacing and/or an epinephrine infusion 2-10 mcg/min (dopamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min is an alternative).
Call for help and the code cart early — before the rhythm degenerates.
On asystole: declare the arrest, start CPR immediately, and give epinephrine 1 mg IV every 3-5 minutes; asystole is not shockable.
Run 2-minute CPR cycles with minimal interruptions; use EtCO2 to gauge compression quality (below 10 mmHg suggests inadequate compressions) and watch for the abrupt EtCO2 rise of ROSC.
Search for reversible causes in parallel: persistent vagal stimulus, hypoxia, hypovolemia, embolism, anesthetic overdose.
After recovery, agree on a plan with the surgeon: usually proceed with slow re-insufflation at lower pressure, with atropine drawn up.
Document the event and the rhythm strips, and hand over the episode explicitly at the end of the case.
commonErrors: Waiting for the next NIBP reading to 'confirm' the problem while the ECG rate falls — the cuff lags by up to two minutes.
Treating the number instead of the cause: giving atropine without asking the surgeon to desufflate, so the bradycardia recurs.
Using the old 0.5 mg atropine dose or tiny incremental doses in an unstable patient (ACLS 2020 specifies 1 mg).
Reaching for glycopyrrolate in an unstable bradycardia — its onset is slower and it is not the ACLS agent.
Failing to call for help and the code cart until after the arrest.
Delaying CPR after asystole to fiddle with the monitor, gain lines, or 'double-check' the rhythm.
Attempting to defibrillate asystole instead of running the non-shockable pathway.
Ignoring EtCO2 during CPR, losing both the quality gauge and the earliest sign of ROSC.

points: Peritoneal stretch during insufflation is a classic vagal trigger; young patients with high resting vagal tone and opioid pretreatment are at particular risk.
The fastest treatment for reflex bradycardia is removing the stimulus — desufflation works faster than any drug reaches the circulation.
In cuff-mode monitoring the NIBP is always stale: the ECG rate, pleth amplitude, and EtCO2 are the real-time signals. This is a monitor-literacy point, not a trivia point.
ACLS 2020 bradycardia pathway: atropine 1 mg IV q3-5min (max 3 mg) → transcutaneous pacing and/or epinephrine 2-10 mcg/min or dopamine 5-20 mcg/kg/min.
Asystole is non-shockable: high-quality CPR plus epinephrine 1 mg IV q3-5min, 2-minute cycles, and a parallel hunt for reversible causes.
Capnography during arrest does double duty: compression quality (target above 10 mmHg) and the abrupt sustained rise that announces ROSC.
Crisis resource management determines speed: declaring the problem out loud, calling for help early, and assigning compressions/drugs/timer roles.
The decision to resume surgery after a vagal arrest is a joint one — slower insufflation, lower target pressure, and vagolytics drawn up are standard precautions.
questions: What told you the heart rate was falling — and how long would you have waited for the next cuff reading?
Why does desufflation work faster than atropine here? What does that imply about treating reflex bradycardias generally?
What is the current ACLS atropine dose and ceiling, and what are your next two moves if it fails?
At what point did this rhythm become an arrest, and what had to happen in the first 10 seconds afterward?
What was your EtCO2 during compressions, and what would a value of 8 mmHg have told you?
How did the team divide roles once help arrived, and what would you script differently?
Would you let the surgeon re-insufflate after recovery? Under what conditions?
